label: ai
topic: A lifestyle app that tells people what to do gains cultish popularity
text: The pastel lilac logo of Abelique sat on the home screens of both Sarah and Chloe, glowing upward into their faces like the ambient light of a new, benevolent god. We were sitting at our usual Thursday lunch table at a bistro that used to serve excellent truffle fries, but Sarah had requested a table by the window to maximize her exposure to midday lux levels, per the app's rigorous decree. I was halfway through a glass of unoaked Chardonnay when Chloe abruptly shifted in her chair, straightening her spine until her vertebrae audibly popped. Her phone had just emitted a soft, Tibetan singing bowl chime. That was the Abelique posture notification. It monitored the gyroscope in her pocket to ensure she wasn't slumping. I asked her if she wanted a sip of the wine, and she looked at the glass as if it were a chalice of medical waste. Abelique had determined her optimal hydration window had closed at eleven-forty, and she was currently in a digestive fasting sequence until dinner, which the app had pre-ordered for her: a bowl of steamed kelp and a single hard-boiled egg. Sarah, meanwhile, was frantically swiping through a list of approved foreign cinema. Abelique had analyzed her latest heart rate variability data and decided she needed to watch exactly forty-two minutes of a slow-paced French drama to lower her cortisol before her designated sleep cycle began at eight-fifteen. I took a deliberate, defiant bite of my sourdough bread. It was exhausting to be the only unoptimized biological entity left in our friend group. I suggested we talk about the impending layoffs at the firm, or perhaps the weather, but Chloe was busy logging the ambient humidity. Abelique's cultural saturation had been a slow, insidious creep. First, it was just an invite-only beta testing program whispered about in yoga studios. Then, the influencers started posting their Abelique wellness scores. Now, you couldn't walk into a coffee shop without hearing that damned singing bowl chime. I thought I could compartmentalize the insanity, keep it contained to my lunch hours, but the contagion had breached the workplace. On Tuesday, Greg, a middle-management acolyte whose previous wellness initiative involved replacing all the breakroom chairs with exercise balls until Brenda from accounting blew out her knee, summoned us for a mandatory synergy meeting. He stood at the head of the conference table, his phone plugged into the projector, displaying his own Abelique dashboard. His sleep architecture was a vibrant array of green bar graphs. He announced that the company had secured a corporate partnership with Abelique. Participation was technically voluntary, but Greg made it abundantly clear that our quarterly performance reviews would heavily factor in our holistic optimization quotients. He told me, with a perfectly straight face, that my tendency to schedule client calls during my circadian dip was negatively impacting the team's energetic alignment. I looked around the room, expecting to meet the eyes of a fellow skeptic, someone who would roll their eyes at the dystopian absurdity of an algorithm dictating our REM sleep and our carbohydrate intake. But the room was a sea of subtly nodding heads. Even Brenda, still wearing a knee brace, was downloading the app. I walked back to my desk, my phone heavy in my pocket. Greg had already sent out the installation links. I sat down and watched a pigeon outside my window, a creature blissfully unaware of its own cortisol levels, eating a piece of discarded trash with absolute, unoptimized joy. I opened my phone and, out of sheer, petty rebellion, ordered a massive, double-shot, full-dairy mocha, fully aware that Abelique, hovering in the digital ether, was already judging me.
